title: A History of English Romanticism in the Eighteenth Century
description: A History of English Romanticism in the Eighteenth Century — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "A History of English Romanticism in the Eighteenth Century" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Alexander Pope
memes.description: The presiding poet of English Augustan classicism, whose perfected heroic couplet became the "reigning mode" against which the Romantic revival defined itself.

memes.label: Anne Radcliffe
memes.description: English novelist whose Gothic romances, notably "The Mysteries of Udolpho" (1794), perfected the machinery of terror-and-explanation that defined the late eighteenth-century Gothic school.

memes.label: Antiquarianism
memes.description: The learned study of the material and textual remains of the past — buildings, records, coins, and old ballads — that Beers calls the indispensable engine of the medieval revival

relations.label: CHAPTER VI. The School of Warton
CHAPTER VIII. Percy and the Ballads

memes.label: Blank Verse
memes.description: The unrhymed measure that, in Beers's account, became "a shibboleth of romanticizing poets" — the Miltonic alternative to the heroic couplet, restored by Thomson and the nature poets against Augustan taste.

memes.label: Bristol
memes.description: "The old west-country city 'of the bridge', birthplace of Chatterton and setting of his invented medieval Bristol."

memes.label: Thomas Chatterton
memes.description: English poet and literary forger (1752–1770), "the marvelous boy" of Bristol, whose Rowley forgeries make him, for Beers, the tragedy at the heart of the history of English romanticism.

memes.label: Chivalry
memes.description: The feudal, knightly manners and romance-matter of the Middle Ages whose recovery — above all in Hurd's "Letters on Chivalry and Romance" — supplied the romantic revival with one of its richest imaginative fields.

memes.label: Clara Reeve
memes.description: English novelist and critic whose "The Old English Baron" (1778) was among the earliest imitations of Walpole's Gothic romance, and whose "The Progress of Romance" (1785) contributed to the emerging theory of prose fiction.

memes.label: Classicism
memes.description: The eighteenth-century pseudo-classic or Augustan taste — order, correctness, and authority — the reigning mode against which the romantic revival was a protest.

memes.label: Edinburgh
memes.description: "The Scottish capital and seat of the literati, epicentre of the Ossian controversy and of eighteenth-century ballad scholarship."

memes.label: Edmund Spenser
memes.description: Elizabethan poet (1552–1599), author of The Faërie Queene — "the poet of romance" whose stanza and manner became the standard of the eighteenth-century Spenserian revival.

memes.label: Edward Young
memes.description: English poet (1683–1765), author of Night Thoughts and the influential Conjectures on Original Composition — a link between the Augustan age and the graveyard school, and a stimulus to the German romantics.

memes.label: Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard
memes.description: Thomas Gray's 1751 elegy — "the most admired and influential poem of the generation" — the summit of the mid-century poetry of melancholy and the graveyard mood.

memes.label: Eton College
memes.description: "The school on the Thames whose 'distant prospect' gave Gray one of his most admired odes and knit together the Gray–Walpole–West circle."

memes.label: Fingal
memes.description: James Macpherson's 1762 "ancient epic," presented as a translation from the Gaelic of Ossian — the central text of the Ossian controversy and of eighteenth-century literary forgery.

memes.label: 'Front Matter'
memes.description: 'E-text prepared by Jeanette Hayward and Al Haines.'

memes.label: Gilbert West
memes.description: English poet and translator of Pindar whose Spenserian imitations and Druidic imagery contributed to the early romantic revival of medieval and primitive themes.

memes.label: Gothicism
memes.description: The eighteenth-century taste for the "Gothic" — medieval manners, architecture, and romance — which the classical age despised and which Hurd defended as peculiarly "suited to the ends of poetry."

memes.label: Gottfried August Bürger
memes.description: German poet whose ghastly ballad "Lenore" became the most translated German poem in English and whose art was forged by Percy's "Reliques."

memes.label: Grongar Hill
memes.description: John Dyer's 1726 octosyllabic landscape poem — "a pictorial poem, a sketch of the landscape seen from the top of his favorite summit in South Wales" — an early anticipation of Wordsworth's love of hills.

memes.label: Hagley
memes.description: "Lord Lyttelton's Worcestershire park, a showpiece of the new natural garden and a shrine to Thomson, poet of 'The Seasons'."

memes.label: Horace Walpole
memes.description: English man of letters, antiquary, and novelist (1717–1797), builder of Strawberry Hill and author of The Castle of Otranto — a central figure of the eighteenth-century Gothic revival.

memes.label: James Beattie
memes.description: Scottish poet and essayist (1735–1803), author of The Minstrel and a Dissertation on Fable and Romance — a late figure in whom several strands of romantic influence are twisted together.

memes.label: James Macpherson
memes.description: Scottish poet and literary forger (1736–1796), author-"translator" of the Ossianic poems, whose Fingal and Temora launched the Ossian controversy and gave Europe a new fashion of primitive, melancholy epic.

memes.label: James Thomson
memes.description: Scottish poet (1700–1748), author of The Seasons and The Castle of Indolence — the founder of the eighteenth-century "return to nature" and the age's chief poet of landscape.

memes.label: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe
memes.description: The sovereign figure of German literature, whose "Götz" and "Werther" shaped the Sturm und Drang and whose ballad art returned to England with the German tributary.

memes.label: John Dyer
memes.description: Welsh poet and landscape painter (1699–1757), author of Grongar Hill — one of the earliest Spenserians and a painter-poet whose love of hills anticipated Wordsworth.

memes.label: Joseph Addison
memes.description: The "Spectator" essayist and classical arbiter of taste who condemned "Gothick" wit, yet praised the old ballads and mapped the "pleasures of the imagination."

memes.label: Joseph Ritson
memes.description: Antiquary and editor whose scrupulous textual scholarship challenged Percy's editorial liberties and laid the foundations of modern ballad editing.

memes.label: Joseph Warton
memes.description: English poet, critic, and clergyman whose "Essay on Pope" (1756) helped shift taste from Augustan classicism toward the Romantic revival.

memes.label: Landscape Gardening
memes.description: The "natural school" of English garden design that rose beside descriptive poetry — freedom and irregularity against the formal French garden, a parallel to the romantic return to nature

relations.label: CHAPTER VI. The School of Warton

memes.label: Landscape Poetry
memes.description: The eighteenth-century poetry of external nature and rural scenery — the "return to nature" of Thomson, Dyer, and Shenstone — whose naturalism shared a root with the romantic revival.

memes.label: Letters on Chivalry and Romance
memes.description: Richard Hurd's 1762 critical letters defending the "Gothic manners and fictions" as above the classic for poetry — a founding document of romantic criticism.

memes.label: Literary Forgery
memes.description: The mid-century fashion for forged "antique" poetry — Macpherson's Ossian and Chatterton's Rowley — by which, in Beers's phrase, antiquarianism became poetry and the romantic revival found some of its most influential texts.

memes.label: Mark Akenside
memes.description: English poet and physician whose "Pleasures of Imagination" (1744) was a landmark didactic poem of the mid-eighteenth century, blending Spenserian and Miltonic influences.

memes.label: Matthew Arnold
memes.description: Victorian poet and critic frequently cited by Beers as an authoritative voice on genuine poetry, Homer, Ossian, and the Celtic element in English literature.

memes.label: Matthew Gregory Lewis
memes.description: Novelist and balladeer, "Monk" Lewis, who pushed Gothic romance to a sensational extreme and funneled German terror into English literature, briefly mentoring the young Walter Scott.

memes.label: Medievalism
memes.description: The 18th-century revival of interest in the Middle Ages — the reproduction in modern art of medieval life and thought — which Beers takes as the very core of romanticism.
aliases: the return to the Middle Ages

memes.label: Naturalism
memes.description: Leslie Stephen's third current beside sentimentalism and romanticism — the eighteenth-century "return to nature," twin-rooted with romanticism in the revolt against Augustan artificiality

memes.label: Night Thoughts
memes.description: Edward Young's blank-verse meditation on death (1742–44) — the "funereal sable" extreme of the mid-century graveyard poetry, hugely influential on the Continent.

memes.label: Northern Antiquities
memes.description: The mid-century recovery of Norse and Runic antiquity — Mallet's Edda, Gray's "Descent of Odin" — which opened to English poetry a pre-classical Northern mythology and fed the romantic revival's taste for the primitive and the sublime.

memes.label: 'CHAPTER IX. Ossian'
memes.description: 'In 1760 appeared the first installment of MacPherson''s "Ossian."[1] Among those who received it with the greatest curiosity and delight was…'

memes.label: Oxford
memes.description: "The university city whose Bodleian and Ashmolean collections made it the antiquarian home of the Warton school."

memes.label: Paul-Henri Mallet
memes.description: Swiss scholar and historian whose "Northern Antiquities" introduced Eddaic and Runic mythology to eighteenth-century England and helped fuel the Gothic revival.

memes.label: 'CHAPTER VIII. Percy and the Ballads.'
memes.description: 'The regeneration of English poetic style at the close of the last century came from an unexpected quarter.'

relations.label: The Minstrel, or the Progress of Genius

memes.label: Poetic Diction
memes.description: The Latinized, periphrastic, personified language the classical school held proper to verse — the "spurious poetic diction" that the romantic reform set itself against

relations.label: CHAPTER VI. The School of Warton
CHAPTER VIII. Percy and the Ballads

memes.label: Primitivism
memes.description: 十八世纪对"原始、质朴、自发"的向往——对过度文明与雕琢的反动，推崇早期与民间诗歌的天然力量。

memes.label: Reliques of Ancient English Poetry
memes.description: Thomas Percy's 1765 collection of old ballads, songs, and early poetry — the single most important title of the decade 1760–1770 and the great channel of the ballad revival into English romanticism.

memes.label: Richard Hurd
memes.description: English critic and churchman (1720–1808), author of the Letters on Chivalry and Romance, whose defense of "Gothic manners and fictions" helped to initiate the romantic movement.

memes.label: Robert Blair
memes.description: Scottish poet and clergyman (1699–1746), author of The Grave — a blank-verse poet of the churchyard whom Beers counts among the "heavy didactic-moral" forerunners rather than the romanticists proper.

memes.label: Romanticism
memes.description: The reproduction in modern art or literature of the life and thought of the Middle Ages, and the eighteenth-century revival of that spirit.

memes.label: Sainte-Palaye
memes.description: French medieval scholar whose "Mémoires sur l'ancienne Chevalerie" and "Histoire de Troubadours" supplied foundational antiquarian learning for the Gothic revival in England and France.
aliases: Jean-Baptiste de La Curne de Sainte-Palaye

memes.label: Samuel Johnson
memes.description: The pre-eminent critic of the English Augustan age, whom Beers casts as the "classical watch-dog" resisting the Spenserian, Miltonic, and balladic tastes of the revival.

memes.label: Sentimentalism
memes.description: The 18th-century cult of feeling, melancholy, and tender emotion — one phase of the revolt against Augustan coldness that fed the romantic revival.

memes.label: Spenserianism
memes.description: The eighteenth-century revival of Edmund Spenser — his stanza, diction, and allegory — which by mid-century had become "the fashion" and which Beers reads as a metrical and imaginative opening toward the romantic revival.

memes.label: St. Mary Redcliffe
memes.description: "The late-Gothic parish church at Bristol whose muniment room and 'antique script' parchments inspired Chatterton's Rowley forgeries."

memes.label: Stonehenge
memes.description: "The prehistoric stone circle on Salisbury Plain, emblem of the Druidic and antiquarian sublime prized by the Warton school."

memes.label: Strawberry Hill
memes.description: "Horace Walpole's mock-Gothic villa at Twickenham, cradle of the Gothic Revival and birthplace of 'The Castle of Otranto'."

memes.label: The Alps
memes.description: "The Alpine mountains, whose sublimity on the Grand Tour helped teach the age to feel 'so much beauty with so much horror'."

memes.label: 'CHAPTER II. The Augustans'
memes.description: 'The Romantic Movement in England was a part of the general European reaction against the spirit of the eighteenth century.'

memes.label: The Ballad Revival
memes.description: The 18th-century recovery and imitation of old English and Scottish popular ballads, led by Percy's Reliques, a major current of the romantic revival.

memes.label: The Castle of Indolence
memes.description: James Thomson's 1748 Spenserian poem — the one imitation of Spenser that "caught the glow and splendor of the master," possessing the "romantic iridescence" absent from Augustan verse.

memes.label: The Castle of Otranto
memes.description: Horace Walpole's 1764 Gothic romance — "perhaps the first modern work of fiction which depended for its interest on the incidents of a chivalrous age" — and the prototype of the Gothic novel.

memes.label: The Elegy
memes.description: 十八世纪的哀歌诗体，以格雷《墓园哀歌》为顶点，将退隐、忧郁与死亡沉思固化为浪漫主义前夜的主导情调。

memes.label: The Faërie Queene
memes.description: Edmund Spenser's Elizabethan romance-epic — "the typical work of the English Renaissance" and the standard of the eighteenth-century Spenserian revival, the antithesis of Pope's art.

memes.label: 'CHAPTER XI. The German Tributary'
memes.description: 'Up to the last decade of the eighteenth century the romantic movement in Great Britain had been self-developed and independent of foreign…'

relations.label: Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard

memes.label: 'CHAPTER VII. The Gothic Revival.'
memes.description: 'One of Thomas Warton''s sonnets was addressed to Richard Hurd, afterward Bishop of Lichfield and Coventry, and later of Worcester.'

memes.label: The Gothic Romance
memes.description: The prose-fiction genre inaugurated by Walpole's "Castle of Otranto" — the haunted-castle romance whose apparatus of ghosts, feudal tyrants, and subterranean vaults passed from Reeve and Radcliffe to Lewis and Scott

memes.label: The Grave
memes.description: Robert Blair's 1743 blank-verse poem of the churchyard — "mortuary verse" of "gloomy monotony" that, with Young's Night Thoughts, gave the graveyard school its "funereal sable."

memes.label: The Hebrides
memes.description: "The stormy Western Islands of Scotland, a locus of the northern and Celtic sublime for the poets of the romantic revival."

memes.label: The Heroic Couplet
memes.description: The closed, rhymed, decasyllabic couplet — the classical school's standard verse, whose displacement marked the romantic revival

memes.label: The Scottish Highlands
memes.description: "The mountainous north of Scotland, homeland of Ossian and a defining landscape of the sublime for the romantic revival."

memes.label: The History of English Poetry
memes.description: Thomas Warton's three-volume literary history (1774–1781) — "the greatest service" the antiquarian scholar rendered to the study of early English poetry, a foundation of the medieval revival.

memes.label: The Lake Country
memes.description: "The mountainous north-west of England whose scenery Gray's 'Journal in the Lakes' opened, three decades before Wordsworth."

memes.label: 'CHAPTER IV. The Landscape Poets'
memes.description: 'There is nothing necessarily romantic in literature that concerns itself with rural life or natural scenery.'

relations.label: Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard

memes.label: The Leasowes
memes.description: "William Shenstone's Shropshire farm, laid out as a 'ferme ornée' and a famous early monument of the landscape-garden taste."

memes.label: 'CHAPTER V. The Miltonic Group'
memes.description: 'That the influence of Milton, in the romantic revival of the eighteenth century, should have been hardly second in importance to Spenser''s…'

relations.label: Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard

memes.label: The Minstrel
memes.description: James Beattie's unfinished Spenserian poem (1771–74) in which "several strands of romantic influence are seen twisted together" — melancholy, landscape, elegy, and Ossianic mountain-love.

memes.label: The Ode
memes.description: The mid-century revival of the lyric ode — from Cowley's "pseudo-Pindaric" to the finished odes of Collins and Gray — one of the formal signs of the return of imagination and music to English poetry.
aliases: the odes of Collins and Gray

memes.label: The Picturesque
memes.description: The taste for freedom, variety, irregularity, and naturalness in gardening and scenery that fed the romantic revival.

memes.label: The Poetry of Melancholy
memes.description: The mid-century vogue for pensive, elegiac melancholy — Milton's "Il Penseroso" recast as a poetic mood of retirement, twilight, and the tomb — one of the sentimental tributaries of the romantic revival.

memes.label: The Rowley Poems
memes.description: Thomas Chatterton's pseudo-medieval poems, attributed to a fictitious fifteenth-century monk Thomas Rowley — the forgery through which, in Beers's phrase, "antiquarianism became poetry."

memes.label: 'CHAPTER VI. The School of Warton'
memes.description: 'In the progress of our inquiries, hitherto, we have met with little that can be called romantic in the narrowest sense.'

memes.label: The Schoolmistress
memes.description: William Shenstone's 1742 Spenserian poem — "an affectionate half-humorous description" of a village dame-school, which Gray pronounced "a masterpiece in its kind."

memes.label: The Seasons
memes.description: James Thomson's blank-verse poem of nature (1726–1730) — the founding work of the eighteenth-century "return to nature" and the great model of English landscape poetry.

memes.label: The Sonnet Revival
memes.description: The mid-eighteenth-century recovery of the sonnet — a form "disappeared entirely" under Pope's couplet — led by Gray, Edwards, and the Wartons in the wake of the Miltonic revival

memes.label: The Spenserian Stanza
memes.description: 《仙后》所用的九行诗节，十八世纪被重新拾起以对抗英雄对偶句，成为斯宾塞复兴的形式载体。

memes.label: 'CHAPTER III. The Spenserians'
memes.description: 'Dissatisfaction with a prevalent mood or fashion in literature is apt to express itself either in a fresh and independent criticism of…'

memes.label: 'CHAPTER I. The Subject Defined'
memes.description: 'To attempt at the outset a rigid definition of the word _romanticism_ would be to anticipate the substance of this volume.'

memes.label: The Sublime
memes.description: The aesthetic category of grandeur, awe, and terror — "the sublime and the wonderful" — that the Warton school opposed to Augustan correctness and that drew the age toward mountains, ruins, and the poetry of imagination.

memes.label: 'CHAPTER X. Thomas Chatterton.'
memes.description: 'The history of English romanticism has its tragedy: the life and death of Thomas Chatterton--"The marvelous boy, The sleepless soul that…'

memes.label: Thomas Gray
memes.description: English poet (1716–1771), author of the "Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard" and pioneer of the Norse revival, whose feeling for ruins, mountains, and the Lake Country prefigured the romantic movement.

memes.label: Thomas Percy
memes.description: English antiquary and editor (1729–1811), compiler of the Reliques of Ancient English Poetry and translator of Northern Antiquities — the man who did most to bring the old ballads back into English literature.

memes.label: Thomas Tyrwhitt
memes.description: Chaucerian editor and classical scholar who first published Chatterton's Rowley poems and exposed the forgery through linguistic analysis.

memes.label: Thomas Warton
memes.description: English critic, antiquary, and poet (1728–1790), author of the History of English Poetry and enthusiast of Gothic architecture — a leading scholar of the medieval revival.

memes.label: Victor Hugo
memes.description: French Romantic poet, novelist, and dramatist whose works and critical prefaces defined European Romanticism and provided Beers with a recurring comparative reference for the English romantic revival.

memes.label: Wales
memes.description: "The Welsh hills, castles, and bardic tradition that fed the landscape poetry, the Celtic revival, and the Gothic taste."

memes.label: Walter Scott
memes.description: Scottish poet, novelist, and ballad collector whose early translations of German Romantic poetry and later Waverley novels mark the culmination of the eighteenth-century Romantic revival traced by Beers.

memes.label: William Collins
memes.description: English lyric poet (1721–1759), "among the choicest of English lyrical poets," whose odes — including the "Ode on the Superstitions of the Highlands" — mark a romantic advance in imagination and music.

memes.label: William Mason
memes.description: Poet, dramatist, and friend and editor of Gray, whose imitative verse and Celtic-Norse experiments made him a minor but characteristic figure of the mid-century revival.

memes.label: William Shenstone
memes.description: English poet and landscape gardener (1714–1763), author of The Schoolmistress and creator of the Leasowes — a figure of the age's elegiac melancholy and love of retirement.

memes.label: William Taylor of Norwich
memes.description: Translator and critic who first brought Bürger's "Lenore" into English and, more than any man of his generation, spread knowledge of German literature in England.

memes.label: Windsor
memes.description: "The royal forest and castle on the Thames — subject of Pope's conventional nature-poetry and the boyhood spur to Warton's antiquarianism."
